data.title: Tectonic Therapeutic: A $1B+ Opportunity Hinges On Upcoming Phase 2
data.description:
Tectonic Therapeutic (TECX) is reviving relaxin biology. Target indication is Group 2 Pulmonary Hypertension (PH) in the presence of Heart Failure with Preserved Ejection Fraction (HFpEF). They are trying to build commercial and clinical success out of a pathway that was abandoned due to lack of clinical success (Eli Lilly). AstraZeneca ran their relaxin program through phase 2b, where it failed.

There are examples in biopharma where such revivals have happened. We saw two blockbuster drugs develop from PCSK9 inhibition biology despite early skepticism. Another set of super blockbusters came about in GLP-1 agonism. Tectonic is attempting the same thing.

And they have early data to prove the underlying biology. Although just phase 1b, hemodynamic improvements seen in the trial in a small set of patients follow directly from the known physiological effects of relaxin. It should reduce pulmonary vascular resistance, improve cardiac output, and improve left heart function—all of whose early signs came across in the data. Importantly, prior attempts with relaxin failed to consistently show just this kind of effect.

Lead candidate TX45 has been developed by engineering a long-acting Fc-relaxin fusion. The aim is to overcome the short half-life that limited earlier programs. It also targets a more well-defined patient population where there’s more likelihood of the biology working successfully. That has resulted in a dataset that doesn’t look like noise. More clear proof should come with phase 2 toplining in 2026. That is an important crossroads for the company.

If TX45 works, this is a multi-billion-dollar underserved market. There are prospects of label expansion. If it works, this could be the beginning of a platform—if it doesn’t, the entire edifice collapses, with nothing left. Standard early-stage biopharma investing.

Understanding TX45: Why This Biology Could Work

Relaxin is a natural hormone present in the human body in low levels. In pregnant women, when the fetus needs more oxygen and nutrients from the mother’s body, relaxin “relaxes” the vascular system and makes both the heart and the lungs deliver more oxygen and nutrients. It also helps pelvic ligaments expand during delivery. These dual aspects—vascular relaxant with indirect hemodynamic effects and antifibrotic effects—have long been hypothesized to “offer therapeutic potential in the treatment of cardiovascular disease.”

Relaxin acts through the RXFP1 receptor. One important effect of RXFP1 activation is that it does not get switched off due to repeated use, so there’s potential for longer-duration therapeutic effect. This is a factor that can be used to our advantage. Earlier relaxin programs did demonstrate adequate improvements—in renal blood flow, pulmonary pressure, and heart activity improvements. But they required continuous IV infusion because the respective drugs—just like natural relaxin—got rapidly cleared from the body. So the effect was not durable enough and did not adequately translate to clinical benefit.

TX45 directly addresses this problem at a molecular level. In the ISHLT 2025 poster, the author says the following:

Both an initial Fc–Relaxin-2 fusion protein (TX-04) and the published Fc–Relaxin construct (RELAX0010), as described in the patent WO2021255127A1, showed an exaggerated alpha-phase decline in rats (6). We hypothesized that this may be due to their high isoelectric points (pI > 9) and corresponding high heparin binding. In response, we developed TX45 by strategically reducing its positive charge, lowering the pI to ~7.8, which decreased heparin binding and improved the pharmacokinetics in rats.

What this means, in plain language, is that the company thinks that earlier Fc-relaxin constructs failed due to high isoelectric point (pI > 9). This caused unwanted heparin binding and rapid clearance. When TX45PL was reduced, it reduced this heparin interaction so that it allowed the molecule to remain in the body for longer, enhancing the therapeutic effect. Company material introduces multiple preclinical benefits of this; I will not go into that because it is too technical and unnecessary in an investment report.

But the bottom line is that the company’s phase 1 SAD poster confirms this preclinical pharmacology is carried into humans. TX45 shows a longer half-life of 14-20 days, so intermittent dosing is possible. PK (pharmacokinetics: what the body does to the drug in terms of ADME – absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion) rises proportional to dose, and SC (subcutaneous, easy injection) dose shows ~50% bioavailability - basically meaning that the drug lasts in the body long enough to show therapeutic effect, unlike natural or previously developed relaxin.

Takeaway: Relaxin biology was always valid. The problem was with the drug’s long-term residence within the body. That led to inadequate therapeutic benefit. TX45 addresses that. Phase 1b data shows as much.

The Trial Data: What It Shows

The phase 1b trial had 19 patients (PH-HFpEF/HFmrEF). From the patient PVR and PCWP data, we can see that these were typical PH-HFpEF populations, not cherry-picked. ~50% of patients had CpcPH (CpcPH is defined as PVR ≥ 2 WU), explained below. Here’s the data from a single-dose exposure:

There are two types of this disease: IpcPH, where the problem is mainly from the left heart backing up pressure, and CpcPH, where both the left heart and damaged/tight lung blood vessels create the problem. TX45 works by relaxing blood vessels, so if the problem is tight lung vessels, like in CPCPH, it should work, but if the problem is just back pressure from the heart (IpcPH), then relaxing vessels won’t help much. In the trial, we saw big improvement in CpcPH patients and not much in the IpcPH patients. That is actually good because the drug works in the subgroup where it is supposed to work. That means improvement was not random.

Improvements were also durable. On various metrics like LVEF and RV function, improvements were still measurable one month later. The trial was robustly designed because the whole effect was confirmed in a second, similar cohort. There wasn’t a single one-off signal. In this cohort as well, the same PCWP, PVR and cardiac output improvements were robust and consistent. The safety profile was solid, with no major adverse events, a minor drop in blood pressure due to vasodilation, no HF worsening, and no major lab issues.

My takeaway: data was early but robust; this was not noise but a real signal that came from a well-designed, small, early-stage, short-duration trial. Phase 2 should provide confirmation.

The Market Potential Discussion

The company discusses the market potential in the corporate deck, the TD Cowen transcript, and the 10-K. The company frames Group 2 PH-HFpEF as a large and underserved market with no approved therapies and some 1.0–1.4 million U.S. patients. The higher-risk CPC-PH subgroup—more clinically important because of robust data—has some 300,000 patients. Expansion opportunity in PH-ILD comes with about 60,000 U.S. patients and significant unmet need.

For pricing, I looked elsewhere. This resource shows pricing of broad chronic CV/HF drugs—so, for example, Entresto’s average WAC per 30-day supply was $656 in 2024, or about $7,872 per year. Most patients pay far less out of pocket for Jardiance, but US WAC comes to around $6,600 per year.

Next, specialty pulmonary hypertension drugs. Liquidia's Yutrepia has a WAC of $24,360 per 28-day supply—priced similarly to Tyvaso DPI — so that’s around $317,000 annually. Merck’s Colorado pricing sheet shows Winrevair priced at $30,596.36 per kit. Annual costs vary by body weight and usage but sit in the higher range of specialty PH drugs.

From this, we get our range: TX45, being an SC biologic, once marketed, should sit nearer to broad HF/CV drugs, but at a much higher price point. So the most reasonable modeled launch range for TX45 in Group 2 PH-HFpEF is:

This places TX45 above standard HF oral drugs, which is reasonable for a novel biologic with no approved therapy in the target setting. But it also keeps it real, far below orphan PH drugs, since the addressable population is so much larger. If forced, I would put a single-number base case price of about $40,000 per year WAC in PH-HFpEF. In PH-ILD, I think the model can support a higher price—something like $60,000–$100,000 per year.

Revenue Build

In ~300K US patient CpcPH, at $40k/yr pricing, I get the following numbers:

  • 5% penetration — $600M

  • 10% penetration—$1.2B

  • 15% penetration—$1.8B

That gives me a base case peak sales range of $0.6B–$1.8B (U.S., PH-HFpEF only). The bull case could easily double these numbers. Admittedly, given the lack of approved drugs, my penetration scenarios are conservative.

Valuation

Assuming a 30% probability of success and a 3x peak sales multiple, risk adjusted values I get are:

  • Low: $600M × 3 x 30% = $540M

  • Base: $1.2B × 3 x 30% = $1.08B

  • High: $1.8B × 3 x 30% = $1.62B

Assuming a 2028 launch and peak sales in ~2032-2033, I will time-discount this by 10-12% per year. Applying a ~50% discount factor for 6-7 years out, I get present values of:

  • Low: $270M

  • Base: $540M

  • High: $810M

In terms of upside, ex-US expansion could add ~50-75% of the US market, so we can add:

  • Low: +$300M peak—~$135M PV

  • Base: +$600M peak—~$270M PV

  • High: +$1B peak—~$450M PV

PH-ILD could add another $135M-$270M to the PV. I have assumed a 60k population, $60k pricing, and 10-20% penetration, which gives me peak sales of $360M–$720M. Applying the same 3x sales multiple but a 25% probability of success because of the earlier stage and a 50% time discount, I get those PV values.

Total Valuation (TX45)

Financial Summary

Tectonic’s current market cap is $526M. Cash balance is $253.8M as of Dec 2025. Debt is negligible.

Cash used in operations has been between ~$13M and ~$18M per quarter since about 2024. That’s roughly $55M–$70M per year, annualized. This is corroborated by the quarterly operating loss figure. This is ~$18M–$22M and includes R&D at ~$13M–$17M and SG&A at ~$5M. Capex is negligible, so we will use operating burn as a clean proxy of total cash burn.

That gives us a runway of 3.5-4.5 years. We expect higher burn after phase 2. However, current cash will be easily able to fund phase 2. If that turns out well, I expect enough price improvement to let TECX raise capital at favorable terms.

Key Risks

Given its relatively early stage, we must account for several risks to any bullish investment thesis.

First, while well-known in theory, from hemodynamic improvements (PVR/PCWP down, CO up) to clinical outcomes is a jump that only clinical data can support. The phase 2 trial must show functional improvements.

Tectonic still has just this single asset in the clinic. If it flops at phase 2, there’s no real fallback option at the same level. That makes Tectonic a binary investment.

There have been several relaxin biology failures. Just in February, AZN’s drug failed. While the company has strong arguments why it should succeed where others failed, it still has to show us.

A safety risk I should touch upon is that a systemic vasodilator like relaxin can cause renal sodium retention as well as plasma volume expansion. In heart failure patients, this is a concern, as Lilly’s patients saw increased HF hospitalizations.

Also note, this is not a rare orphan disease. There are two populations, where I have modeled different pricing for each. If payers push pricing towards the HF range, the PH model will shrink.

As I have also noted, phase 2 has both hemodynamic and clinical endpoints like 6MWD, and 6MWD has no data in phase 1b. So the entire situation is a “prove-it” scenario. In biopharma, this is always risky.

A final key risk worth noting is the relatively high short interest of ~12.5% of float. This shows that a meaningful portion of the market is positioned against TECX. That likely reflects skepticism, including some we have mentioned, like translation of hemodynamic data into clinical outcomes, relaxin’s difficult history, and so on. While high short interest can amplify upside through short covering if data turns positive, it also indicates that the thesis has credible opposing views.

Next Month’s Earnings: What to Expect

Tectonic is expected to report earnings on May 7, 2026. I would keep an eye on management commentary on a possible date or timing of release of phase 2 data, or at least some idea of enrollment progress. I would also stay tuned for updates on cash balance and burn rate, which will tell me whether my range of 3.5-4.5 years is still good. Third—and while this is admittedly early—I would watch out for additional granularity on TX45’s positioning, including early thinking of pricing and target patient population (particularly CpcPH vs. broader PH-HFpEF).

Conclusion and Investment View

Tectonic has solid early data, a decent cash balance, a major upcoming catalyst, and it is targeting a large market with upside from label expansion. These are all the elements pre-commercial biopharma investing is made of. If the data works, my valuation estimates show TECX has considerable upside. Looking at phase 1b data, phase 2 should likely work. However, nothing can be so cleanly said in the biopharma world.

For biopharma investors used to the risk-reward quotient inherent in the space, this is a solid buy at current levels with potential for 2-3x upside from here in the span of 3-4 years, or when phase 3 data comes. I rate it a buy.

data.title: Wealthspire Q1 2026 Review
data.description:
By the Wealthspire Investment Team

It's always difficult to write these letters during periods of geopolitical upheaval. On one hand, we remain steadfast in our belief that geopolitical events rarely require reactive changes in long-term portfolio strategy. On the other hand, the facts steering markets are shifting rapidly, and framing the consequences of war in terms of asset prices can feel inadequate against the impact to entire generations and societies.

Still, markets rarely grant the luxury of pause, and the first quarter of 2026 was a perfect encapsulation of this unfortunate reality. On February 28th , as the United States and Israel launched military strikes on the Iranian mainland, a market that previously found strength from the evolution of the AI capex trade abruptly recalibrated. Overnight, focus shifted toward the vulnerability of the Strait of Hormuz and the economic consequences of its closure. With the market's focus now centered on control of this critical global trade artery, history offers a useful lens for understanding where events may lead next.

Lessons from the Suez Canal

In the fall of 1956, a coalition of Western powers (Britain, France, and Israel) launched Operation Musketeer, a military campaign to recapture the Suez Canal after Egyptian President Gamal Abdel Nasser nationalized the passage. At the time, the canal was the world's most critical maritime chokepoint, serving as the primary artery connecting European markets to Middle Eastern oil and Asian trade. Militarily, Operation Musketeer worked, though strategically it did not. The coalition technically won on the battlefield but lost because of political and economic pressure on the home front. The crisis demonstrated, with painful clarity, that access to a single maritime corridor could carry wide ranging impacts on currencies, government, alliances, and markets. Seventy years later, the United States finds itself in strikingly similar circumstances.

Operation Epic Fury achieved its immediate military aims in Iran with leadership disrupted, missile and drone facilities degraded, and uranium stockpiles reportedly difficult to access by the regime. But the broader strategic goals that these tactical elements signal - an open Strait of Hormuz, more U.S.-friendly Iranian leadership, and regional stability - have not been met. The Strait remains effectively closed, and what was once a potential tail risk scenario has, in the span of weeks, become the largest disruption to world energy supply in decades.

The Hormuz Problem

The economic fallout from Suez was swift and severe, particularly for Britain. The closure of the canal from October 1956 through March 1957 severed the primary shipping lane between Europe and Asia, contributing to oil shortages that rippled across Western Europe. Petrol was rationed and the crisis accelerated the end of British Prime Minister Anthony Eden's government. The financial contagion was compounded by the fact that markets were caught largely off guard with few adequately pricing the risk that a regional military conflict could sever a global trade artery so completely.

Today, the Strait of Hormuz crisis carries comparable, and in some dimensions, greater economic stakes. Before its effective closure, roughly 20 million barrels of oil per day flowed through the strait, representing approximately 18% of global supply, alongside an estimated 20% of global liquid natural gas. 1 The Dallas Fed estimates that this supply disruption is three to five times larger in magnitude than the oil shocks of 1973 or 1990. As a result of its closure, WTI crude oil prices rose 50% from pre-war levels, and non-U.S. oil grades rose by 100% or more over the first quarter. Elsewhere, jet fuel more than doubled year-over-year, and secondary supply chain disruptions spread into plastics, aluminum, fertilizers, synthetic fibers, and industrial chemicals. The widespread impacts underscore that this event carries systemic risk and is not merely an energy event.

The economic toll for such disruption is acute. The Dallas Federal Reserve recently estimated that if the Strait remained closed for the remainder of 2026, it would reduce real global GDP growth by 1.3%, roughly equivalent to Mexico's entire economy going dark for a year.ii

Motivated Resolution

In the U.S., as mid-term elections are nearing, the voting topic du jour remains affordability. In November of last year, elections were held in Georgia, New Jersey, New York City, and Virginia. A consistent theme that emerged was voters' concerns about affordability of housing, energy, food, and the growing cost of everyday life. Exit polling confirmed strategists' suspicions that cost-of-living outranked crime, immigration, and other key topics as voters' top issue. That issue, however, is now colliding with a more complicated economic reality. Gasoline prices and inflation are not abstract policy debates. They are felt at every fill-up and often find their way into voting booths. Like with Britain in 1956, the mechanism for catalyst is less likely to be the battlefield and more likely to be political pressure at home.

The challenge remains finding a credible exit. According to the Associated Press, Iran's terms for peace include per-ship tolls on Strait passage, their right to enrich uranium and pursue a nuclear program, and reparations for damages sustained, among others. These terms suggest Tehran believes leverage is on its side.

What We're Watching

Two signals will define the long-term impact of this conflict.

The first is whether Iran tests its leverage over the Strait of Hormuz. Before this conflict, the idea of an Iranian toll on commercial vessels was not a serious risk scenario. It is now. Nearly 140 ships transit the Strait each day. Iran has floated a $2 million levy per vessel. If implemented, that generates over $100 billion in annual revenue, nearly double its current oil export income of $53 billion.iii The financial incentive is real, and a permanent toll regime would redirect global commodity flows toward Chinese-aligned economies and create lasting friction for Western markets.

The second signal is the extent of damage to energy infrastructure. A swift resolution could reopen the Strait within weeks, but physical damage to production and refining facilities is a different problem. Repairs take months, sometimes years. If supply is forced to contract structurally with no credible alternative to fill the gap, the inflationary pressure this conflict already created may not resolve when the fighting does.

Green Shoots

The stakes are high, but on balance, we see genuine reasons for measured optimism. Earnings growth is broadening, powered by AI and defense spending. The Bloomberg Economic Surprise Index sits near its highest level since 2023, and shelter costs, a stubborn driver of core inflation, continue to moderate. Critically, a soft labor market reduces pressure on the Fed to respond to inflationary pressure by tightening financial conditions. Collectively, these conditions buy time for a diplomatic resolution and create a backdrop under which one can succeed.

U.S. Economic Surprise Index

Source: Bloomberg Finance, L.P. As of April 1, 2026.

The Suez Crisis resolved in weeks once political will collapsed under domestic pressure. We believe this conflict has the potential to follow a similar arc. The forcing function exists, the motivation is acute, and the economic backdrop remains resilient enough to absorb the shock while a resolution takes shape. As a result, we are remaining disciplined and watching evolving conditions closely. Risks remain, chiefly around infrastructure damage and willingness to negotiate in good faith. But it's important to note that both the market and the economy entered 2026 with considerable momentum, providing insulation against any lasting economic impacts stemming from the conflict.

Markets

  • Global stocks retreated in March and the first quarter after a strong start in January and February. The S&P 500 ended the quarter down 4.3%, and the Nasdaq Composite fell 6.9%, their worst quarter since 2022. The Iran conflict dominated sentiment from its onset on February 28th . The quarter closed on an encouraging note as green shoots of resolution emerged, and the S&P 500 surged 2.9% on March 31st , its best single trading day of 2026.iv
  • In the U.S., technology was the hardest-hit sector. Software stocks pulled back on concerns that AI investment would erode their competitive value. Over the last five years, software as a service traded at a 50% premium to the broader market.v That premium has been erased over the last two quarters. The weakness drove a broad rotation into value stocks across every market-cap tier. Large-cap growth lost 9.8%, while large-cap value gained 2.1%. Small-cap value led all segments, rising 5%, as investors sought defensively positioned, attractively priced companies.
  • International stocks held up well by comparison, though March returns softened as the Iran conflict came into focus. For the quarter the MSCI EAFE fell just 1.2% and MSCI Emerging Markets slipped only 0.2%, both comfortably outpacing U.S. markets.
  • Bond markets offered modest stability. The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index was flat, TIPS rose 0.3%, and high yield bonds dipped just 0.5%, cushioned by income. Rates climbed through the quarter on inflation concerns tied to higher energy prices. Markets have largely erased expectations for a Fed cut in 2026, a sentiment we think may be overdone in the short term.

data.title: Integrating ESG Into A Flagship U.S. Equity Benchmark: The Russell 1000 Blossom Indexes
data.description:
Environmental, social and governance (ESG) considerations have evolved from niche allocations to core drivers of investment decision-making. As a result, investors are increasingly focused on how to integrate sustainability insights into their primary portfolio exposures—without compromising alignment to widely used benchmarks.

The Russell 1000 Blossom Index and the Russell 1000 Blossom Sector Relative Index combine two well-established FTSE Russell structures—the Russell US Indexes and the FTSE Blossom methodology—to identify large-cap US companies with stronger ESG practices. The combination delivers core US equity exposure while minimizing any industry or sector bias and incorporating ESG considerations in a transparent and systematic way.

The Russell 1000® Index: a flagship benchmark for US equities

Launched in 1984, the Russell US Indexes provide a comprehensive, modular framework for representing the US equity opportunity set. The indexes are capitalization-weighted and float-adjusted 1, enabling them to capture approximately 99% of the US equity market’s investable market capitalization. More than $11 trillion in assets are benchmarked to Russell US Indexes across asset management, ETF and institutional portfolios worldwide 2.

The large-cap benchmark of the Russell US Indexes is the Russell 1000®, which measures the performance of the largest 1,000 US companies. Several characteristics have helped establish the Russell 1000 Index as a widely adopted benchmark:

  • Transparent and objective methodology – Constituents are determined using clear, rules-based criteria rather than discretionary selection.
  • Comprehensive market representation – The index captures the largest companies within the broader Russell 3000® Index universe, providing broad coverage of the US large-cap segment.
  • Predictable semi-annual reconstitution – Russell US Indexes are reconstituted twice each year, ensuring the benchmark reflects ongoing changes in market capitalization and company growth.
  • Investability and liquidity – Float adjustment and liquidity screens help ensure the index remains suitable for index-tracking products and institutional portfolios.

Building the Russell 1000 Blossom indexes

The FTSE Blossom Index Series was originally developed for the first ESG index-tracking fund in Japan, run by Japan’s Government Pension Investment Fund (GPIF). The FTSE Blossom Japan index was designed to identify Japanese companies demonstrating stronger ESG practices, while maintaining neutrality in industry weights relative to the capitalization-weighted market benchmark.

In 2025, FTSE Russell extended the Blossom methodology to select indexes of global stocks, choosing the Russell 1000 as the starting universe for the US equity market. The Russell 1000 Blossom Index and the Russell 1000 Blossom Sector Relative Index integrate sustainability considerations into a flagship US large-cap benchmark, while preserving the broad market characteristics investors expect from a core equity allocation.

The methodology for the Russell 1000 Blossom indexes draws on FTSE Russell ESG Scores, which evaluate corporate sustainability practices across a range of relevant dimensions.

Companies are assessed across three ESG pillars, which are further divided into multiple themes covering issues such as climate change, biodiversity, supply chain labor standards, human rights, health and safety, corporate governance and anti-corruption practices (see Figure 1). These themes are built from numerous underlying indicators that evaluate company disclosures, policies and management practices.

Figure 1. One ESG score, 3 pillars and 14 themes

Using these ESG scores, the Blossom methodology identifies companies that meet defined ESG thresholds while excluding those involved in certain controversial activities or with weaker ESG practices (Figure 2).

For the Russell 1000 Blossom Index, index inclusion is conditional on an overall FTSE ESG score of 3.3 or above. There is a “buffer” between the ESG scores required for index inclusion and exclusion: constituents with an overall ESG score below 2.9 (or having one or more ESG themes assessed as high exposure with a corresponding score of zero) are at risk of deletion from the index. Additional inclusion criteria cover nuclear power generation, climate change score thresholds and high exposure themes.

To incentivize better ESG disclosure and to encourage engagement with potential index members, constituents are allowed a one-year grace period to meet the index eligibility criteria—but will be deleted at the index review one year later if the eligibility criteria are still not met.

As at end-February 2026, the Russell 1000 Blossom index had 431 constituents, compared to 1006 in the Russell 1000 index3.

The Russell 1000 Blossom Sector Relative Index is built slightly differently: it draws stocks from the top 50% of ESG performers, based on FTSE ESG scores, in each ICB sector (subject to a minimum ESG score of 2.0).

In the Russell 1000 Blossom Sector Relative Index, we set another inclusion criterion: a Transition Pathway Initiative Management Quality (TPI MQ) score of 3 or above for the top 10% of greenhouse gas emitters in the index. TPI MQ scores evaluate and track the quality of companies’ governance of their greenhouse gas emissions, and the risks and opportunities related to the low-carbon transition.

As at end February 2026, the Russell 1000 Blossom Sector Relative index had 517 constituents, compared to 1006 in the Russell 1000 index.

The final index construction step in both the Russell 1000 Blossom and the Russell 1000 Blossom Sector Relative indexes ensures industry or sector neutrality: the Russell 1000 Blossom Index is built to ensure neutrality with respect to the ICB industry weightings of the underlying index; in the Russell 1000 Blossom Sector Relative Index, we ensure neutrality with respect to the ICB sector weightings of the relevant underlying index.

Figure 2. Index construction steps

Russell 1000 Blossom Index Russell 1000 Blossom Sector Relative Index
Underlying Index Russell 1000 Index
Exclusion Apply minimum set of exclusions
Stock Selection FTSE ESG Scores >=3.3 and may exclude ESG Scores <2.9*Applicable high exposure theme score >=2.0*Climate Change score of 3 required for primary impact sector, 2 required in secondary impact sector* Top 50% in each ICB sector and FTSE ESG Scores >=2.0TPI MQ score >=3.0 required for top 10% GHG emitters of the underlying universe
Industry / Sector Neutrality Each ICB industry weight is equal to that of the underlying index Each ICB sector weight is equal to that of the underlying index

*If lower scores, at risk of deletion from the index

The methodology of the Russell 1000 Blossom indexes is overseen by FTSE Russell’s ESG Advisory Committee, which includes independent investment professionals with expertise in environmental, social and governance issues. Together with transparent rules and publicly available index documentation, this governance helps support the credibility and consistency of the index methodology.

The Russell 1000 Blossom indexes can serve a range of investment purposes. They may be used as a performance benchmark for ESG-oriented US equity strategies or as the basis for index-linked investment products, including index-tracking funds, derivatives and ETFs. For investors seeking to incorporate ESG considerations while maintaining alignment with a widely used large-cap benchmark, the indexes provide a transparent basis for integrating sustainability insights into core US equity exposure.

To learn more about the Russell 1000 Blossom indexes or other FTSE Russell sustainable investment indexes, please reach out to the FTSE Russell team or explore our latest sustainable investment research and insights.

[1] Float adjustment means we remove restricted shares, shares that are never traded, or shares otherwise not available for purchase from the index to help ensure its liquidity. See Free Float Restrictions (FTSE Russell)

For funds where the AUM was not reported as of December 31, 2024, the previous period AUM was used as an estimate. data.title: JSI: High-Quality MBS ETF, Above-Average Yield, Strong, Short Performance Track-Record
data.description:
The Janus Henderson Securitized Income ETF (JSI) is an actively managed ETF focusing on high-quality, shorter-term securitized assets, including agency MBS, commercial MBS, and ABS. JSI's above-average 6.2% dividend yield, strong, albeit short, performance track record, below-average risk and volatility, and good risk-return profile make the fund a buy.

JSI - Overview and Investment Thesis

Strategy and Portfolio

JSI is an actively-managed ETF focusing on high-quality, shorter-term securitized assets. Allocations are as follows:

With investments in over 500 securities, JSI provides diversified exposure to securitized investments / to the fixed-income sub-asset classes above. JSI does not provide diversified exposure to the fixed-income market as a whole, as it does not invest in several major bond sub-asset classes, including treasuries and corporate bonds (minus that small 1.6% high-yield investment).

JSI's more targeted portfolio means that the fund's characteristics, and hence its performance, could materially differ from that of broader bond benchmarks, including that of the larger bond index ETFs.

Of particular importance is the fact that some of the fund's securitized assets tend to trade at attractive spreads to more traditional fixed-income securities of comparable credit risk. AAA CLOs are the clearest example of this, consistently trading at a spread to T-bills, although these are not one of JSI's larger investments.

Spreads between benchmark mortgage and treasury rates seem to be at slightly historically above-average levels, albeit much lower than in the more recent past. MBS are securitized mortgage investments, so they benefit from above-average mortgage rates/spreads, and JSI invests quite heavily in these.

Risk and Volatility

JSI focuses on high-quality securities, including agency MBS, with an average credit rating of AA. Although the fund focuses on investment-grade securities, it has sizable investments in non-investment-grade assets, with these accounting for 13.8% of the fund's total assets, 16.5% net.

The distinction between net and gross figures is due to JSI's use of leverage. Specifically, the fund sometimes invests in TBA MBS, basically MBS futures. Futures are generally leveraged, with JSI's leverage amounting to around 19.1% of the fund's net assets. JSI sometimes uses derivatives and options, generally as a hedge.

In some cases, accounting leverage does not have a significant effect on a fund's performance. Sometimes the effects of the leverage get hedged out; sometimes accounting leverage is simply an accounting or regulatory issue. JSI's leverage does not seem to have had a significant impact on the fund's past performance, with the fund performing broadly in line with bond and MBS benchmarks since inception, with zero visible increase in risk or volatility. Performance below.

JSI leans short-term, with an average duration of 3.4 years. Most bonds and bond ETFs focus on bonds with longer maturities/durations. As an example, the benchmark Vanguard Total Bond Market ETF (BND) has a duration of 5.8 years, significantly higher than that of JSI.

Expect below-average rate exposure, gains when rates decrease, and losses when these increase. With inception in late 2023, the fund missed most of the past hiking cycle/aggressive rate movements.

JSI's high-quality, shorter-term portfolio should experience below-average volatility and drawdowns, as seems to be the case. Figures for JSI and index ETFs tracking the major bond sub-asset classes below:

JSI focuses quite heavily on MBS and assorted securitized assets, something of a risk factor. Said focus could lead to losses and underperformance if said sector underperforms relative to the broader bond market. MBS performed terribly during the past housing bubble/financial crisis, for instance. Because of JSI's high-quality holdings, I wouldn't expect significant long-term losses or underperformance, although there could be short-term volatility during particularly adverse market events.

In any case, JSI focuses on higher-quality, shorter-term securities, which have resulted in below-average volatility and drawdowns in the past. These are all significant, straightforward benefits for the fund and its shareholders.

Dividend Analysis

JSI sports a 6.2% dividend yield, quite good on an absolute basis, and higher than index ETFs tracking the major bond sub-asset classes, including MBS. JSI's dividends are closer to high-yield corporate bonds than to traditional MBS, even though credit quality is much closer to the latter.

JSI's above-average dividends are due to some combination of weaker credit quality and active management, especially when/due to investing in commercial MBS and ABS. Although these investments aren't too uncommon, most of the larger bond ETFs exclude them, and their yields do tend to be a bit higher than those of treasuries and investment-grade corporate bonds.

Performance Track Record

JSI's performance track record is short, with the fund being created in late 2023, for around 2.5 years of performance data. During that time, JSI has slightly outperformed index ETFs tracking the major bond sub-asset classes, including those targeting MBS and investment-grade corporate bonds.

Outperformance is particularly bullish considering the fact that the fund was created during a small, local rate peak, with rates decreasing/bond prices increasing soon after inception. JSI leans short-term, so it saw much fewer gains than its peers during its first few months of existence but still managed to outperform through steady gains thereafter. Returns are as follows:

JSI has also outperformed short-term investment-grade bond index ETFs, which seems relevant considering its short-term bond focus. Wasn't able to find an appropriate short-term MBS ETF, although I imagine JSI would have seen higher returns than that one too, considering the fund's consistent outperformance of peers.

JSI's outperformance was due to some combination of its higher dividend yield, active management, and, in most cases, its greater credit risk. Do bear in mind, the fund's realized volatility and drawdowns are both below average, so overall risk remains low.

Risk-adjusted returns don't seem all that clear. In my opinion, and based on the data so far, risk-adjusted returns are above average, as the fund compares favorably to most of its peers on both absolute returns and realized volatility/drawdowns. Sharpe and Sortino ratios, however, are both below average.

In my opinion, what other data we have available outweighs the above, so I do believe that the fund's risk-adjusted returns are quite good. It's difficult to conclude otherwise when graphing the fund's performance with that of relevant benchmarks:

Still, the Sharpe and Sortino ratios say otherwise, and these are much more in-depth, quantitative figures. The fund's short track record is an issue here, as it's quite common for there to be lots of short-term volatility and issues with the data/performance. Longer time periods smooth out the volatility, but we don't have long-term performance data for JSI.

JSI - Moving Forward

In my opinion, JSI's strong fundamentals and overall investment thesis will persist moving forward.

The fund's below-average risk and volatility are due to its diversified, higher-quality, shorter-term bond portfolio. ETFs rarely make significant changes to their portfolios. I have no reason to expect such a change from JSI, so expect similar results on this front moving forward.

The fund's above-average 6.2% dividend yield is due to some combination of elevated Federal Reserve rates and attractive spreads (see the previous discussion of MBS and treasury rates). Federal Reserve rates are flat YTD, with the most recent guidance calling for one cut this year. Spreads between MBS and treasuries are down but remain at historically above-average levels.

In my opinion, the fund is likely to see a small decrease in its dividend yield/spreads relative to peers in the coming years. Said decrease is far from certain, and I don't believe it will be all that significant: guidance calls for one rate cut only, and spreads remain reasonably good. As always, conditions could always materially differ from guidance and expectations, as could dividend yields.

JSI's outperformance was due to some combination of its higher dividend yield, which I expect to persist, and active management, which is a bit more uncertain. Still bullish, although obviously active ETFs or strategies sometimes do not work out.

Conclusion

JSI's above-average 6.2% dividend yield and performance track record, below-average risk and volatility, and good risk-return profile make the fund a buy.

data.title: Osisko Development: A $3 Billion Gold Mine You Can Buy For $1 Billion
data.description:
Osisko Development Analysis

I've followed Osisko Development Corp. (ODV) closely for years, for good reason: I own shares of OR Royalties Inc. (OR), one of my most successful investments. Osisko GR holds a 5% NSR royalty on its key asset (the Cariboo Gold Project), plus a pretty significant equity stake in ODV. So, I really want to know everything going on with Cariboo. What I'm seeing right now is a company in a much stronger position to turn a gold project into a producing mine.

Currently, ODV trades at $3.34 and carries a market cap closing in on $1 billion. Cariboo, located in the mining-friendly jurisdiction of central British Columbia, is fully permitted and financed.

At spot gold prices, Cariboo's net present value is several multiples of its market cap, and that disconnect is a big part of the buy thesis, as I explain below.

Osisko Development: What You're Buying

Osisko Development is a North American gold developer led by CEO Sean Roosen, who built and operated the Canadian Malartic mine before selling it to Agnico Eagle (AEM) and Yamana Gold for $4.1 billion in 2014. That track record matters.

Roosen is a proven gold mine builder, and he's also assembled a strong team that has done this before. The 100%-owned Cariboo project is a perfect project for Roosen and his team, as it has the kind of upside worth investing in. It covers a 192,000-hectare land package. And it just so happens to be one of just two fully permitted undeveloped gold mines in Canada, with the other one being Seabridge Gold Inc.'s (SA) KSM project. I think that carries real value in the current permitting environment, where timelines can stretch for 10+ years.

The company also holds the Tintic Project in Utah's historic East Tintic mining district, where it ran small-scale mining activities in 2025 and sold roughly 4,000 ounces in Q4. Tintic is a secondary asset at this point, so I won't spend any time there, as Cariboo is the story.

The Cariboo Economics

Osisko's 2025 feasibility study highlighted the robust economics of Cariboo. The underground gold mine carries an initial 10-year mine life and should produce 190,000 oz Au per year, with a peak of 220,000 ounces.

Reserves stand at 2.07 million ounces. The base case, run at US$2,400/oz gold, generated an after-tax NPV5% of C$943 million with a 22.1% IRR and 2.8-year payback from commercial production.

Those base case numbers are almost academic at this point. When the FS was published in April 2025, gold was around $3,300/oz, and the spot case showed an NPV of C$2.1 billion with a 38% IRR. Gold has since run to nearly $4,800/oz. According to the company's latest corporate presentation, the spot-price NPV now stands at roughly C$3.25 billion, with a 51.9% IRR. The margin expansion at these gold prices is extraordinary.

The cost profile is what really sets Cariboo apart. Feasibility AISC of US$1,157/oz places this project in the lower half of the global cost curve. At a gold price near $4,800, you're looking at implied margins north of $3,600 per ounce. That's the kind of margin that makes a mine incredibly valuable and incredibly resilient to downside scenarios. Even if gold were to retrace to $2,400, the project would still deliver a 22% unlevered IRR. That's a floor most developers would kill for.

Fully Funded For Construction

This is where Osisko separates itself from most development-stage gold companies. The funding gap, which destroys shareholder value at so many juniors, has been largely addressed. As of year-end 2025, the company reported C$422 million in cash. It has a US$450 million project finance facility with Appian Capital, of which roughly US$106 million has been drawn. Additional equity raises in early 2026, including a US$143.8 million prospectus offering and C$24.9 million from warrant exercises, added further liquidity.

Initial capex for Cariboo is estimated at C$881 million. It's not a cheap mine to build. But, between the company's cash on hand, its project facility, and the equity it has already raised, I think the funding picture is pretty clear-cut.

Readers should also note that Osisko appointed JDS Energy & Mining as project and construction manager in February 2026, which signals that the build is moving from planning to execution.

Finally, Osisko was also added to the VanEck Junior Gold Miners ETF (GDXJ) in March 2026, a great development for the stock as it should increase institutional demand for its shares.

The Valuation Disconnect

Here's where this gets interesting. At a market cap of roughly $1 billion, Osisko Development trades at approximately 0.40x its spot-price NAV. Compare that to Perpetua Resources Corp. (PPTA), which trades closer to 0.90x NAV, or Skeena Resources Limited (SKE) near 0.80x.

Osisko's discount is partly explained by the development risk and the dilution from recent capital raises. But those raises are exactly what removed the funding risk. The market hasn't fully re-rated the stock to reflect the improved balance sheet.

Company

Permitting

A permitted, fully funded gold developer trading at 40 cents on the dollar of its NAV, in a Tier 1 jurisdiction, with a management team that has built mines before. I keep circling back to that combination because you don't see it often.

Exploration Upside and M&A Potential

The feasibility study is based solely on 2.07 million ounces of reserves. But Cariboo's mineralized system extends along a 4.4-kilometer strike length with depth potential that has barely been tested. The company has launched a fully funded 160,000-meter exploration program for 2026, targeting new discoveries across the broader land package. Any resource additions beyond the current reserve base would extend the mine life and improve the already strong economics.

I also think Cariboo will be a compelling M&A target once construction is further de-risked. Any North American-focused senior or mid-tier gold miner would find this asset attractive. A company like B2Gold Corp. (BTG), which already operates in Canada through its Back River project and is actively diversifying out of West Africa, would be a natural fit. So would Agnico Eagle Mines Limited (AEM) or Kinross Gold Corporation (KGC), both of which have demonstrated an appetite for permitted Canadian gold projects. Once production is closer to reality, the M&A premium on this name could be material.

Risks to consider

Development risk is the obvious concern here.

The upfront capex figure is pretty large, even though it's reasonable for a project of this size. Also consider the fact that feasibility study estimates often don't come in 100% as estimated and often come in higher. Plus, my builds can be delayed.

Readers should also be aware of a fatal contractor incident at the site in January 2026, which led to a temporary suspension of activities before a phased reopening. That's a sobering reminder that underground construction carries operational risks, and more incidents like that could impact the stock negatively in the future.

Lower gold prices are an obvious risk to consider. This project carries fantastic economics at spot gold, for sure. But a big pullback in gold prices, to, say, $3,000 or lower, would definitely put some pressure on its economics (although I do think it's still a viable project at $2,000/oz gold).

Dilution from recent share issuances is another negative of the stock, but that's also a reality of owning a pre-revenue, pre-cash flow gold developer like Osisko.

Finally, while I like Cariboo as a project, it's still a complex underground operation with execution risk that should not be dismissed.

Bottom Line: Speculative Buy

Osisko Development gives you a fully permitted, fully funded gold development project in one of the world's best mining jurisdictions, run by a team that has done this before at Canadian Malartic. The Cariboo economics at current gold prices are exceptional, with AISC under $1,200/oz, implied margins over $3,600/oz, and an NPV that dwarfs the current market cap by a factor of three or more.

The P/NAV discount I'm seeing compared to similar developers like Perpetua and Skeena is a sign of a solid entry point into the stock. Don't forget about its exploration upside, too, as it continues to explore along its 4.4 km strike. I think there will also eventually be M&A optionality, given the project's strong economics and tier-1 location. I think a market cap of roughly $1 billion is a fair near-term valuation, and I would be a buyer under $3.50 per share.

data.title: UWM Holdings: High-Yield Masks Structural Risk
data.description:
Introduction

UWM Holdings Corporation (UWMC) still remains one of the biggest US companies in the wholesale mortgage market, whose business model is fully oriented to the independent broker channel. Unlike the main competitors, UWMC completely refuses direct loaning to consumers; the company's profitability directly depends on brokers loyalty and technological advantage in the process of issuing loans.

2025 data shows that the company generated $2.8 billion in revenues, though net profit margin dropped to 12.4% due to ongoing price wars in the sector. The current non-GAAP P/E ratio of 13.5 is below the five-year historical average; the current share price doesn't reflect the growth in fundamental value.

Even though dividend yield is reaching 10.7%, the payout ratio is exceeding 300%, which limits the company's ability to reinvest in technological expansion without additional borrowing. The company's business efficiency remains at the top of the sector, with the average loan origination cost at $1850, though this operational advantage is neutralized by the high sensitivity to interest rate fluctuations.

Strategic concentration in only one segment increases systematic risk, because any broker regulation tightening directly cuts through the UWMC revenue base, and the 2026 forecast suggests that there will only be a 3% growth in loan volumes.

Business overview

UWM Holdings Corporation takes up a dominating part in the US market as a mortgage lender that operates solely through the wholesale channel. The company currently controls more than 54% of this market, working with independent brokers and completely abandoning the retail service model.

In 2025, total loan issuance volumes reached $163.4 billion, which was a 17% growth compared to 2024. The business model's core is its own technological ecosystem, including the EASE platform and AI assistant Mia, which in 2026 should process over 12 million calls.

This ensures operational efficiency, allowing us to close loans in 14-18 days, when the market's average is over 40 days. An essential strategic breakthrough happened earlier this year, when the company successfully carried out a product rotation from purchase to the refinancing segment. If in 2024 purchase loans were dominating, now in 2025 Q4, refinancing volumes reached $30.7 billion and, for the first time in a few years, were above purchase loans, which made up $18.9 billion.

This rotation was driven by the fall in 30-year fixed interest rates. It allowed customers with expensive loans that were taken out in 2023-2024 to massively refinance liabilities. Even though in 2025 net income dropped to $244 million due to a $435 million revaluation of mortgage servicing rights, in 2026 the revenue forecast was increased to around $3.5-4.5 billion. Further refinancing activity and stable margins, which reached 1.22% at the end of 2025, are expected.

Valuation

UWM Holdings Corporation in 2026 has a valuation that indicates a high risk due to the gap between the share price and the fundamental indicators. The P/E (TTM) ratio at around 30.6x is well above historical multiplier averages for this sector, which are usually in single digits. For comparison, the main competitor, Rocket Companies (RKT), is traded at a negative or a very high P/E ratio, though UWMC's forward P/E is at around 18.6, which suggests that the market is expecting an aggressive profit recovery in 2026.

P/B ratio is reaching around 5.5, which is well above the sector's average of around 1.1. This shows that investors are paying a high premium for the company's technological advantage and Mia Ai platform integration. Dividend yield is reaching 10.7%, though the payout ratio reaching 300% is a clear sign that the dividends are not covered by profits and raises serious sustainability questions. Compared to PennyMac Financial Services (PFSI), whose valuation multipliers remain more moderate, UWMC seems overvalued.

Though the company maintains leadership in this segment and is forecasting revenues of $3.5-4.5 billion in 2026, the current price doesn't reflect the high debt-to-equity ratio of around 9, which creates additional financial pressure. The planned acquisition of Two Harbors and the move to an in-house servicing model in 2026 are being tried to justify as a tool for margin expansion, though for now it doesn't reflect in net income margin, which is reaching just 1.76%. Such a valuation doesn't leave much room for errors, because any revenue forecast discrepancy could cause a quick multiplier contraction to the sector's median.

The newest releases about the Two Harbor deal even further weaken the investment thesis. Two Harbors Investment Corp. terminated an earlier deal with UWMC and chose a $10.80 per share cash offer from CrossCountry Mortgage.

This shows that in the market, transaction certainty and instant liquidity were more valued than UWMC’s offered share-based deal, which may have nominally appeared more attractive.

This raises questions about how the market values UWMC's share price quality and reliability. If strategic partners choose a smaller but guaranteed cash offer instead of UWMC shares, this suggests that the company’s future results, integration, or valuation are associated with a higher risk than the management communication suggests.

Risks

UWM Holdings Corporation is facing a critical dividend sustainability risk, which is indicated by the very high payout ratio exceeding 300% from the net income. Even though a 10.7% dividend yield is attractive, the payout ratio shows that the company isn’t paying dividends from its operating returns, but rather from accumulated capital or increasing indebtedness. This directly correlates with a high financial leverage; the debt-to-equity ratio is reaching around 9, which limits the company's flexibility in the case of a potential market drop.

Another significant risk is with the mortgage servicing rights portfolio’s valuation, which at the end of 2025 made up a big part of the assets. MSR value is very sensitive to interest rate changes: if in the second part of 2026 market interest rates drop more quickly than expected, a wave of early loan repayments will cause MSR depreciation, which will negatively impact the net income through paper losses. Operational risk remains high due to the very low net income margin, which at the start of 2026 was at 1.76%. Such a low margin leaves a minimal space for mistakes with the increasing cost of client acquisitions or the increasing competitiveness with Rocket Companies.

Finally, investors need to assess the management's risk, where the CEO controls a big part of the voting rights through SFS Corp. His active share selling in 2026 is sending a negative signal to the market about the internal company assessment.

Thesis summary

In my view, UWM Holdings Corporation at first glance seems like an attractive high-yield company, but looking deeper, it's more of a risk company than an upside. The business is strong in an operational sense, the efficiency is high, the broker's channel is dominating, technological advantage exists. But the problem is that this model is fully dependent on interest rate cycles and the broker's activity, which are not controllable factors.

Dividends look attractive, but the payout ratio exceeding 300% clearly shows that they are not sustainable. This is not an income play; it's a temporary yield, which could be adjusted if the cycle doesn't help. Also, the very high financial leverage and the MSR portfolio’s sensitivity to interest rates create additional pressure on profitability, which is already not looking great.

Valuing the multipliers the market already has priced in an aggressive profitability recovery; therefore, there is not a lot of margin of safety. Any deviation from the forecasts could quickly pressure the valuation.

data.title: Rheinmetall: A Multi-Combat System Provider At The Start Of A NATO Spending Super Cycle
data.description:
In August 2025, I analyzed Rheinmetall AG (RHM:CA, RNMBY, RNMBF), citing it as one of the best-positioned companies to benefit from NATO’s increased spending targets. However, the shares have largely remained flat as investors focused on various peace initiatives regarding the Ukraine invasion that could lead to a decline in ammunition sales, as well as on a high valuation and lackluster results. I now update the view, cutting the YE26 price target to E$1,844 from E$2,400 due to slower backlog conversion and new contract awards as governments move far more slowly.

Buoyant backlog, slower revenue conversion

RHM does not have a backlog problem; since 3Q25, it has increased its backlog by $26 billion, or 32%, which adds greater long-term value to the company and should have led to a positive share price reaction. The problem is the speed at which these contracts are converted into revenue. In 3Q25, the consensus assumed over 20% of backlog would be converted into revenue; by the end of 1Q26, this had dropped to 12% for 2026, and only in 2028 did it reach 20%. This has driven forecasted revenue down by nearly $3 billion a year, which in turn cuts into earnings and valuation.

There are several reasons for this delayed revenue or lower conversion ratio, including slower government procurement processes, logistics bottlenecks, and growing pains. Any industrial operation facing large new orders needs to add capacity, machinery, and personnel. It's generally a good problem to have, but it can cause missed guidance and higher costs.

Operating Model

In the last year, RHM has added several key combat systems, including naval, air, and ballistic missile capabilities, moving up the value chain and expanding its strategic position as a defense contractor for NATO. The operating model forecasts over 30% consolidated revenue growth led by digital solutions. EBITDA should grow at a rate of 40% through 2028, driven by scale gains and the adoption of higher-value-added systems, many in JVs with US defense companies seeking to mitigate NATO local content requirements.

Valuation

As with most stocks, I utilize an adjusted earnings estimate I call cash earnings (CE), which is net income, depreciation, and stock-based compensation, as the principal metric. I then value this stock relative to the CE per share growth. In the RHM case, the consensus has an E$2,087 price target with a P/CE of 43.9x or 1.1x PEF vs. the next three years' average growth. This may be entirely achievable, but I prefer to use a 1x PEG and arrive at an E$1,844 price target for YE26. Note that as the company increases its backlog-to-revenue conversion, it should maintain a high CE growth rate, and, longer term, the shares could nearly triple in my view.

Peer Comps

I gathered a broad array of peer comps from consensus data to gauge growth and valuation for the defense sector. In general, there has been a significant increase in forecasted growth for most, as geopolitical events have spurred higher budgets and now the replacement of equipment that has been spent or destroyed. RHM is the second-fastest-growing company in this sector, with adjusted earnings growth over 40%, while its PEG, or relative to growth ratio, is under 1x vs. over 2x at the larger US companies.

NATO Spending Increase

The main driver for RHM and all European and Canadian defense companies is the established goal to increase spending from 2.3% of GDP to 5% by 2035. Not all countries may abide, and many will factor in circumspect spending to meet targets, but the main economies—Germany, France, the UK, Poland, and Canada—should more than double defense spending. About 25% of this goes to equipment, where RHM and others are providers, and from this 25%, I assume 40% and rising will be won or allocated to EU/UK/Canadian companies and increase as they gain aerospace capabilities, such as ballistic missiles and drones, or develop robots, etc. This scenario should lead to an annual increase of over 10% in orders and constitute a massive super cycle for the sector. This was at first incentivized by the Ukraine invasion and the USA’s reluctance to continue to subsidize NATO. The second Trump government and the Iran War further push the NATO alliance to seek security independence from the US.

Risk

The first risk to near-term results would be the end of the Ukraine invasion and no replenishment of ammunition, etc., which is about $2.2 billion in current backlog. A more relevant risk is that Germany and NATO backtrack from the 5% GDP spending target or extend it to a 20-year target, for example. And as we have seen, red tape, operating bottlenecks, and supply chain problems can also reduce revenue and earnings.

Conclusion

I rate RHM a buy. The company has positioned itself as a key provider of multi-combat systems at the start of an EU/German defense spending surge, which could lead to a 4x increase in earnings by 2029. At the same time, the shares are not overvalued, trading at under 0.6x PEG, and may re-rate as RHM delivers on its increasing backlog.
